coredata.dc:title: Solid phase synthesis of nitrogen and phosphor co-doped carbon quantum dots for sensing Fe3+ and the enhanced photocatalytic degradation of dyes
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yongming
Cao, Fengpu
Li, Yabo
coredata.dc:description: A facile and one-step strategy has been developed for the preparation of highly fluorescent nitrogen and phosphor co-doped carbon quantum dots (NP-CQDs) through the thermal treatment of the mixture of citric acid and O-phosphorylethanolamine. The as-prepared NP-CQDs emit strong blue-green light under irradiation of 365nm light and show emission maxima at 475nm in aqueous solution. And these NP-CQDs exhibit great stability in high-salt conditions, excellent photostability and a high quantum yield of about 8.45%. Interestingly, the NP-CQDs have been successfully utilized for the sensitive and selective fluorometric detection of Fe3+ without interference from other ions in aqueous solution. Moreover, we have found that the NP-CQDs can enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 for the degradation of dyes under simulated sunlight irradiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid phase synthesis
Carbon quantum dots
Fe3+
Photocatalytic degradation
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in tap water with NP-CQDs
Photocatalytic degradation of MB with NP-CQDs
Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
Study of photocatalytic activity of NP-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the possible formation process for NP-CQDs.
(A) XPS spectrum of NP-CQDs. (B) FT-IR spectrum of NP-CQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of NP-CQDs, inset: Photographs of NP-CQDs under daylight (a) and UV light (b, excited at 365nm). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of NP-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs. (B) Comparison of fluorescence intensities of N,P-CQDs in the presence of different metal ions (performed in pH 6.0, 50mM MES buffer; the concentration of metal ions is 200μM; the concentration of F− is 1mM; excitation wavelength is 360nm).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of NP-CQDs in the presence of different concentration of Fe3+. (B) The plot of I/I0 versus the concentration of Fe3+, inset: the linear relationship between I/I0 and Fe3+ concentration (performed in pH 6.0, 50mM MES buffer; I0 and I correspond to the fluorescence intensity of NP-CQDs at 475nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively; excitation wavelength is 360nm).
(A) TEM image of TiO2. (B) TEM image of NP-CQDs/TiO2. (C) Comparison of photocatalytic performance of NP-CQDs/TiO2, TiO2 and NP-CQDs.

head.ce:title: Solid phase synthesis of nitrogen and phosphor co-doped carbon quantum dots for sensing Fe3+ and the enhanced photocatalytic degradation of dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanyang Normal University, Nanyang 473061, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly fluorescent nitrogen and phosphor co-doped carbon quantum dots (NP-CQDs) with great stability in high-salt conditions, excellent photostability and a quantum yield of about 8.45% have been prepared through the thermal treatment of the mixture of citric acid and O-phosphorylethanolamine. The NP-CQDs can be utilized for the sensitive and selective fluorometric detection of Fe3+ and enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 for the degradation of dyes under simulated sunlight irradiation.

A facile and one-step strategy has been developed for the preparation of highly fluorescent nitrogen and phosphor co-doped carbon quantum dots (NP-CQDs) through the thermal treatment of the mixture of citric acid and O-phosphorylethanolamine. The as-prepared NP-CQDs emit strong blue-green light under irradiation of 365nm light and show emission maxima at 475nm in aqueous solution. And these NP-CQDs exhibit great stability in high-salt conditions, excellent photostability and a high quantum yield of about 8.45%. Interestingly, the NP-CQDs have been successfully utilized for the sensitive and selective fluorometric detection of Fe3+ without interference from other ions in aqueous solution. Moreover, we have found that the NP-CQDs can enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 for the degradation of dyes under simulated sunlight irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, fluorescent nanomaterials consist mainly of inorganic and organic fluorescent nanomaterials that have attracted tremendous attention due to their unique properties and promising applications in numerous fields including environmental, material and life sciences [1–6]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a newly emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, have shown significant advantages in good biocompatibility, low-cost, tunable emission, excellent photostability, high quantum yield, ease of fabrication [4,6]. Up to date, many strategies have been well developed for the preparation of various CQDs, such as hydrothermal method, electrochemical method, microwave-assisted method, solid phase synthesis method and so on [7]. Among these reported methods, solid phase synthesis method is highly desirable because the method does not require any solvent, which is very green and safe.

Recently, there is increasing interest in the preparation and application of hetero-atom doped CQDs because the doped hetero atoms can adjust the structure and composition of CQDs, resulting into the change of their properties. Zhang et al. have proposed one-pot solvothermal approach for synthesizing nitrogen-doped CQDs. The obtained CQDs exhibited tunable fluorescence emission due to the different nitrogen content [8]. However, this method is dangerous because CCl4 and NaNH2 were used as starting materials. Bourlinos et al. have recently prepared boron-doped CQDs via the microwave heating of an aqueous solution of citric acid, boric acid and urea. The as-prepared CQDs displayed a significant enhancement of the nonlinear optical response in comparison with the un-doped CQDs [9]. You’s group has synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs by one-step microwave-assisted method. The nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs showed small particle size, uniform surface state, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and the fluorescence signal is insensitive to excitation wavelengths and environmental conditions when compared with nitrogen-doped CQDs [10]. Similarly, various nitrogen and phosphorus-doped CQDs (NP-CQDs) have been successfully prepared via hydrothermal and microwave-assisted methods [11–13]. These methods might exhibit a little bad repeatability because the amount of water showed great effect on the repeatability of hydrothermal or microwave-assisted method. Therefore, new methods for the synthesis of NP-CQDs are urgently needed.

In this study, a simple and facile method was proposed for synthesizing stable NP-CQDs with a bright blue-green emission, which was performed by one-pot thermal treatment of citric acid and O-phosphorylethanolamine. The obtained NP-CQDs with an absolute quantum yield of 8.45% exhibited great photostability and excellent stability in high-salt conditions. We have discovered that the as-prepared NP-CQDs can be used to selectively and sensitively detect Fe3+ in aqueous solution. Thus, a simple and selective nanosensor for Fe3+ has been established. In addition, we have investigated the catalytic performances of NP-CQDs, which can greatly enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB). The study demonstrated that the NP-CQDs have potential applications in environmental and analytical chemistry fields.

Citric acid (CA) and titanium butoxide were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Inc. (Shanghai, China). O-phosphorylethanolamine (PEA) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). Other reagents were all of analytical reagent grade and used as received without further purification. Double-distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained at room temperature on a Lambda 650S UV–vis spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo-Electron Corp., USA) with the KBr pellet technique in the range of 400–4000cm−1. The excitation and emission spectra were measured using a Cary Eclipse luminescence spectrometer (Varian, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) spectra were measured at an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope operated at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield were measured using a FLS 980 spectrofluorimeter (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). Photostability tests were carried out at room temperature using the light of 365nm from an ultraviolet lamp (16W, CBIO-UV3A, Beijing CBIO Bioscience Technology Co. Ltd., Beijing, China). Photographs were obtained using a Canon 700D digital camera.

In a typical procedure, CA (about 210mg) and PEA (about 148mg) were mixed together and added into a Teflon-lined autoclave, which was heated at a constant temperature of 200°C for about 12h. After cooling to room temperature, a dark solid material was obtained and dissolved in water with assistance of ultrasound. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 10000rpm for about 15min to remove large particles. The brown supernatant was collected and dialyzed in a cellulose ester dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 500Da against distilled water at room temperature for about 24h. Finally, a clean and brown solution containing NP-CQDs was obtained and stored at 4°C. The concentration of NP-CQDs was estimated to be about 0.4mg/mL by weighing the dried NP-CQDs.

750μL 2-(N-Morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid buffer solution (MES, 0.2M, pH 6.0), NP-CQDs solution (200μL) and different concentration of Fe3+ were mixed together. And water was added into the mixture to ensure that the total volume was 3.0mL. After incubation for about 10min at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of NP-CQDs was recorded with the fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in tap water with NP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water was collected in our laboratory and was filtered through a 0.22μm filter to remove any suspended particles. Tap water (60μL) was mixed with different concentration of Fe3+ and 750μL MES buffer (0.2M, pH 6.0). Then NP-CQDs (200μL) were added into the mixture and water was used to dilute the above mixture to final volume of 3.0mL. After incubation for about 10min at room temperature, the fluorescence signal of NP-CQDs was recorded with the fluorescence spectrometer.

For the synthesis of NP-CQDs/TiO2, NP-CQDs (1.75mL) was first mixed with water (18.25mL) and stirred for about 5min. 1.2mL titanium butoxide was dropped into the above mixture, and the mixture was stirred for about 30min. Then, the mixed solution was then sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave, which was put into an oven at 160°C and maintained for about 24h. The autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature, and the resulted products were collected by centrifugation and washed with water and ethanol. The obtained NP-CQDs/TiO2 was dried at 60°C for about 24h. The synthetic method for TiO2 is similar to the NP-CQDs/TiO2, but without the addition of NP-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of MB with NP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance was studied through the decolorization of a model pollutant MB dye under simulated sunlight conditions. To study the photocatalytic reaction, 10mL of MB solution (about 20mg/L) was mixed with 30mL water, and 80mg NP-CQDs/TiO2 was added into the above mixture. The obtained mixture was subjected to ultrasonic dispersion for about 10min. Before illumination, the reaction system was stirred in the dark for about 30min to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The solution was magnetically stirred and exposed to simulated sunlight irradiation with a 300W Xenon lamp (Beijing Perfectlight, PLS- SXE300C). At certain time intervals, 3mL of the solution was collected and analyzed by measuring the absorbance of MB at 665nm with the UV–vis spectrophotometer. For comparison, the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 and NP-CQDs was also tested under the identical conditions to the NP-CQDs/TiO2 samples.

We proposed a simple one-step method for synthesizing NP-CQDs through the thermal treatment of CA and PEA at 200°C. We first prepared CQDs via the thermal treatment of CA, PEA and the mixture of CA with PEA at 200°C, respectively. Three brown CQDs solutions were gained. The two diluted solution of CQDs prepared from CA and PEA showed blue color in UV light of 365nm. However, the NP-CQDs from the mixture of CA and PEA exhibited bright blue-green fluorescence under 365nm UV light irradiation (Fig. S1). And the excitation spectra and emission spectra of the three CQDs solutions were quite different. For the CQDs from CA, the maximum excitation wavelength and maximum emission wavelength were 320nm and 425nm, respectively (Fig. S2). The CQDs from PEA showed the maximum excitation wavelength at about 240nm and 340nm, and the maximum emission wavelength was at about 440nm (Fig. S3). However, the NP-CQDs synthesized from the mixture of CA and PEA exhibited the maximum excitation wavelength at about 325nm and 340nm. And the maximum emission wavelength was at about 420nm (Fig. S4). These results demonstrated that the CQDs from the mixture of CA and PEA were different from the CQDs from CA or PEA. Additionally, the reaction was also carried out in a tube furnace under nitrogen protection at 200°C. The original CQDs showed two peaks at about 230 and 340nm in the excitation spectrum. And the emission spectra displayed an excitation-dependent emission behavior with a maximum emission wavelength of 420nm, which is similar to that of the unpurified NP-CQDs prepared in the autoclave (Fig. S5). We chose the reaction in the autoclave because of the simplicity. For the formation of NP-CQDs, we speculated that the amine and phosphoryl groups of PEA may react with the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of CA, respectively. The resulted product finally carbonized into NP-CQDs at high temperature (Fig. 1 ). After dialysis of the NP-CQDs solution, the purified NP-CQDs solution was obtained.

TEM was utilized to characterize the morphology of NP-CQDs. It showed that the NP-CQDs were uniformly dispersed and nearly spherical in shape, and the average diameter was about 3.03±1.01nm (Fig. S6). XPS was applied to determine elemental composition and chemical state of different elements on the surface of NP-CQDs. In the XPS survey spectrum of NP-CQDs, we observed four peaks at around 531.78eV, 400.22eV, 284.5eV, 133.25eV, which were attributed to O1s, N1s, C1s and P2p, respectively (Fig. 2 A). High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s and P2p were presented in Fig. S7. The C1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.57eV, 285.99eV and 288.21eV, which are ascribed to CC/CC, CO/CN and CO/CN, respectively [14–16]. The N1s spectrum revealed two peaks at 400.22eV and 401.42eV, which are attributed to pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [17]. The O1s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 530.34eV, 531.78eV and 533.34eV corresponding to the CO, OCOH, and COH/P-O, respectively [18–20]. The P2p spectrum was fitted to P-O (132.95eV) and (PO4) (133.65eV), respectively [19,21]. The XPS results reveal that the as-prepared CQDs are co-doped with nitrogen and phosphor. FT-IR spectrum was applied to identify the surface functional groups of NP-CQDs. The broad absorption bands at 3400–3500cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibration of OH. The weak peak at 2910cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibration of CH [22,23]. Two strong peaks at about 1700cm−1 and 1772cm−1 corresponding to CO stretching vibration were observed [24]. The peak at 1404cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of CNH [25]. The peaks at about 960cm−1 and 1135cm−1 are related with PO4 (Fig. 2B) [26]. These above results demonstrate that there are abundant of hydroxyl, carboxyl and phosphate groups on the surface of NP-CQDs. And the FT-IR results of NP-CQDs are consistent with the XPS results.

The purified NP-CQDs were well-dispersed in aqueous solution and exhibited brown in daylight. However, the NP-CQDs solution showed bright blue-greenish fluorescence under 365nm UV light (Fig. 3 A). In UV–vis absorption spectrum of NP-CQDs, a peak at about 325nm and a shoulder peak at around 365nm were observed (Fig. 3A). The peak at 325nm may be attributed to n→π* transitions [27]. The excitation spectrum revealed two peaks at approximately 225nm and 360nm. And the peak at about 360nm is very wide (Fig. S8). The fluorescence emission spectra of NP-CQDs were analyzed at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300nm to 420nm. The fluorescence emission spectra showed a slight excitation dependent emission behavior. The maximum emission wavelength shifted from 465nm to 490nm with increasing of the excitation wavelength from 300nm to 420nm. And the strongest emission intensity at about 475nm with an excitation of 360nm was observed (Fig. 3B). The phenomena may be ascribed to the emissions from different size NP-CQDs and multi emissive sites on each NP-CQDs [28]. Fluorescence lifetime measurement revealed that NP-CQDs showed double lifetime with 3.06ns and 10.40ns (Fig. S9). The absolute quantum yield of NP-CQDs was measured to be about 8.45%, which is comparable or better than that of some CQDs and fluorescent metal nanoparticles [2,3,29–31].

The stability of NP-CQDs is of great importance for the practical applications [1–6]. And the stability was studied at pH 6.0. We first investigated the effect of scan times on the fluorescence intensity of NP-CQDs. After consecutive scanning for 20 times, the fluorescence intensity of NP-CQDs remained unchanged (Fig. S10). Photostability of NP-CQDs was also studied by continuous irradiation of the light 365nm. The NP-CQDs exhibited excellent photostability after continuous illumination for about 60min (Fig. S11). Moreover, we found that NP-CQDs had high stability in high-salt condition. 1.5M NaCl showed little effect on the fluorescence intensity of NP-CQDs (Fig. S12). The above results demonstrated that the NP-CQDs displayed excellent photostability and good stability in high-salt condition, which guarantee the potential applications of NP-CQDs in wide fields.

The pH effect on the fluorescent intensity of NP-CQDs has been also studied. We found that pH showed important effect on the fluorescent intensity of NP-CQDs. The fluorescent intensity gradually increased as the pH value increased from pH 4.0 to pH 6.0, and it remained almost stable when the pH value was in a wide pH range of 6–10. And when the pH value was about 6.0, the strongest fluorescent intensity was observed, we thus chose pH 6.0 for the following experiments (Fig. 4 A). This pH dependent fluorescent property revealed that there are many acidic functional groups on the surface of NP-CQDs because low pH value makes the protonation of phosphate groups of NP-CQDs and further causes the decrease of the dispersion of NP-CQDs, resulting into the decrease of fluorescence intensity. This result is consistent with the results of XPS and FTIR.

We then investigated the effect of various metal ions on the fluorescent intensity of NP-CQDs. After the addition of various metal ions with the concentration of 200μM, the fluorescent intensity at 475nm (I475) was used to compare the effect. We found that only Fe3+ and Al3+ could lead to the sharp decrease of I475, revealing the presence of phosphate groups on the surface of NP-CQDs [11]. Simultaneously, the hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of NP-CQDs also preferentially interacted with Fe3+ and Al3+ [12], resulting into the fluorescence quenching of NP-CQDs. Interestingly, F− could mask with Al3+ and had little effect on Fe3+ (Fig. 4B). Meanwhile, excess F− showed no effect on the fluorescent intensity of NP-CQDs (Fig. S13). The results demonstrated that the as-prepared NP-CQDs can be utilized to sense Fe3+ with good selectivity. To obtain the optimum incubation time, we first studied the effect of incubation time on the fluorescent intensity of NP-CQDs in the presence of Fe3+. Upon the addition of Fe3+ with a concentration of 200μM, the I475 decreased greatly and did not change obviously after 1min, revealing that the response rate was very quick (Fig. S14). Therefore, the NP-CQDs can be used as a promising probe for Fe3+ in aqueous solution.

To elucidate the detection mechanism of NP-CQDs for Fe3+, we first studied the UV–vis absorption spectra of NP-CQDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+. The addition of Fe3+ could obviously increase the absorption spectrum of NP-CQDs. To rule out the effect of Fe3+, the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ was subtracted from that of the mixture of NP-CQDs and Fe3+, resulting into a net absorption spectrum, which may really reveal the interaction between NP-CQDs and Fe3+. The net absorption spectrum was still higher than that of the NP-CQDs (Fig. S15), demonstrating that the addition of Fe3+ can change the absorption spectrum of NP-CQDs, which revealed that the detection mechanism of NP-CQDs for Fe3+ is dynamic quenching. Fluorescence lifetime measurement was further applied to study the detection mechanism. Upon the addition of 200μM Fe3+, the NP-CQDs exhibited a single fluorescence lifetime with 9.05ns (Fig. S16), which is different from that of the NP-CQDs (Fig. S9), demonstrating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism caused by Fe3+ is dynamic quenching [32,33]. The above results indicated that the detection mechanism of Fe3+ is dynamic quenching, which resulted from the selective interaction of Fe3+ with the phosphate and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of NP-CQDs. Moreover, the nonradiative electron/hole recombination annihilation may also result into the fluorescence quenching of NP-CQDs [11].

Under the optimized conditions, the sensitivity of the NP-CQDs for Fe3+ was evaluated by adding different concentration of Fe3+ into the NP-CQDs solution, and the corresponding fluorescence response was recorded. With increasing of Fe3+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of NP-CQDs gradually decreased, and the shape and position of the fluorescence emission peak did not change (Fig. 5 A). A plot of I/I0 against the Fe3+ concentration was obtained, and a good linearity was observed in the range of 0.05–200μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9975 (Fig. 5B). The lowest detection concentration of Fe3+ is 0.05μM, which is much lower than the maximum allowable level (0.3ppm, about 5.4μM) for Fe3+ in drinking water set by the United States Environmental Protection Agency [22]. The performance of the NP-CQDs for sensing Fe3+ is comparable or even better than those reported results (Table S1) [12,28–31,34–37]. The above results revealed the NP-CQDs could be used as an excellent candidate for the determination of Fe3+ in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of NP-CQDs as a probe for Fe3+ was evaluated with tap water. Tap water was first filtered with a 0.22μm membrane to remove any suspended particles. The addition of tap water did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the NP-CQDs, indicating the concentration of Fe3+ in tap water may be less than 0.05μM (Fig. S17). Different concentration of Fe3+ was added into the tap water, and the fluorescence intensity of NP-CQDs gradually decreased (Fig. S18A). I/I0 of the NP-CQDs decreased with increasing amounts of Fe3+, and a plot between I/I0 and the Fe3+ concentration can be set up. A linear relationship ranging from 0.05 to 200μM was also obtained with a correlation coefficient of 0.9967, demonstrating the NP-CQDs are feasible for the determination of Fe3+ in environmental samples (Fig. S18B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of photocatalytic activity of NP-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: We also studied the photocatalytic activity of NP-CQDs. We first prepared NP-CQDs/TiO2 and TiO2 nanoparticles through hydrothermal method. TEM images revealed the NP-CQDs did not obviously change the morphology of TiO2 nanoparticles, and the size of NP-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles is slightly smaller than that of TiO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 6 A and B). Under the same conditions, the photocatalytic activity of them was estimated through the decolorization of a MB dye under simulated sunlight conditions. It is easily observed that NP-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles showed better adsorption performance to MB than TiO2 nanoparticles and NP-CQDs, 40% MB had been adsorbed by NP-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles in dark for 30min, which may be attributed the strong adsorption ability of rich acidic functional groups on the surface of NP-CQDs embedded in TiO2 nanoparticles. And the NP-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles also had good photocatalytic activity towards MB, and cause the complete degradation of MB in 15min (Fig. 6C). Such a high photocatalytic activity of NP-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles may be ascribed to the separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs and fast carrier migration assisted by NP-CQDs [38]. However, the mechanism has not been fully elucidated and further study is needed. The results clearly demonstrated that the NP-CQDs might enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 towards MB.

In conclusion, we have developed a simple and facile method for the solid phase synthesis of stable NP-CQDs with a bright blue-green emission. The as-prepared NP-CQDs showed a high quantum yield of 8.45% and excellent stability in high-salt conditions. And we have found that the NP-CQDs can be used to selectively detect Fe3+ in aqueous solution with a linear range from 0.05–200μM. In addition, we have investigated the photocatalytic performances of NP-CQDs, which can greatly enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanoparticles for photocatalytic degradation of dyes. The study demonstrated that the NP-CQDs have potential applications in environmental and analytical chemistry fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21401113) and Startup Foundation for Advanced Talents of Nanyang Normal University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.08.104.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemistry, biology, and medicine of fluorescent nanomaterials and related systems: new insights into biosensing, bioimaging, genomics, diagnostics, and therapy
Fluorescent copper nanoparticles: recent advances in synthesis and applications for sensing metal ions
Stable fluorescent gold nanoparticles for detection of Cu2+ with good sensitivity and selectivity
Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions
Soft fluorescent nanomaterials for biological and biomedical imaging
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles for the fluorescent detection of metal ions
One-pot synthesis of N-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Green and simple route toward boron doped carbon dots with significantly enhanced non-linear optical properties
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg (II) ions
Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots as a novel fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ in human serum and living cells
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen and phosphorus doped carbon dots for the detection of Fe3+ ions in cancer cells
One-pot and ultrafast synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots possessing bright dual wavelength fluorescence emission
Facile access to B-doped solid-state fluorescent carbon dots toward light emitting devices and cell imaging agents
A facile microwave-assisted fabrication of fluorescent carbon nitride quantum dots and their application in the detection of mercury ions
Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped graphene quantum dots: synthesis from adenosine triphosphate, optical properties, and cellular imaging
Phosphorus, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for real-time measurement of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species inside macrophages
Selenium doped graphene quantum dots as an ultrasensitive redox fluorescent switch
Microwave-assisted synthesis of N,P-doped carbon dots for fluorescent cell imaging
Facile synthesis of P-doped carbon quantum dots with highly efficient photoluminescence
Kinetic study of the oxidation resistance of phosphorus-containing activated carbons
Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for Fe3+ sensing and cellular imaging
Amino acid functionalized blue and phosphorous-doped green fluorescent carbon dots as bioimaging probe
A promising electro-oxidation of methyl-substituted aromatic compounds to aldehydes in aqueous imidazole ionic liquid solutions
pH-sensitive carbon dots for the visualization of regulation of intracellular pH inside living pathogenic fungal cells
N,P-codoped carbon networks as efficient metal-free bifunctional catalysts for oxygen reduction and hdrogen evolution reactions, Angew
Synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots for the detection of cobalt ions
Synthesis of high fluorescence graphene quantum dots and their selective detection for Fe3+ in aqueous solution
One step synthesis of functionalized carbon dots for the ultrasensitive detection of Escherichia coli and Iron (III)
Comparative study for N and S doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization and applications for Fe3+ probe and cellular imaging
Glutathione capped silver nanoclusters-based fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+
Thermal treatment of hair for the synthesis of sustainable carbon quantum dots and the applications for sensing Hg2+
Hydrothermal synthesis of blue-emitting silicon quantum dots for fluorescent detection of hypochlorite in tap water
Liquid nitrogen-assisted synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from Blueberry and their performance in Fe3+ detection
Green synthesis of carbon dots from rose-heart radish and application for Fe3+ detection and cell imaging
Fluorescent oligomer as a chemosensor for the label-free detection of Fe3+ and dopamine with selectivity and sensitivity
Synthesis and evaluation of a new furfuran-based rhodamine B fluorescent chemosensor for selective detection of Fe3+ and its application in living-cell imaging
Modulation and effects of surface groups on photoluminescence and photocatalytic activity of carbon dots

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yongming Guo obtained his Ph.D. degree in chemistry from Chengdu Institute of Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2013. Now he is a lecturer at College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering of Nanyang Normal University. His research interest involves the synthesis and applications of functional nanomaterials.
Fengpu Cao is a lecturer at College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering of Nanyang Normal University.
Yabo Li is an undergraduate at College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering of Nanyang Normal University.
